observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our previous recommendations that the City Mayor issue a directive to all the Department Heads who are in-charge of the implementation of the PPAs of NGAs, to:

complete the implementation of the delayed/unfinished programs and projects;

evaluate the feasibility of its continuance and inform the Source Agencies of any problems/difficulties on its implementation, otherwise return the funds to the national government, if not viable; and

submit the Purchase Requests and/or Program of Works promptly to the City Bids and Awards Committee to commence the procurement process.

We also recommended that the City Mayor should write the Source Agencies of funds, wherein the implementation of the PPAs have been completed, to inform the NGA concerned of the City’s intention to retain the unutilized amount totaling ₱1,257,222.67 to support the City’s programs/projects in response to the Covid-19 pandemic pursuant to DBM-DOF-DILG JMC No. 2.

We recommended that the President of the Ormoc City Jackfruit Producers Association procure the items under Package Nos. 4, 7 and 8 and submit the Fund Utilization Report to the OCA for recording in the books of accounts of the City.

We also recommended that the City Engineer and Project Engineer concerned fast track and monitor the completion of the construction of the Multi-Purpose Building within the first semester of CY 2021.

We finally recommended that the Philippine Rural Development Project (PRDP) Focal Person monitor the implementation of all component packages in the Work and Financial Plan and assist the Proponent Group in the execution of the Operational Plan of the Jackfruit Enterprise to ensure that the said enterprise will be fully operational by CY 2021.
observations.managementAction: Construction of 1 unit Multi-Purpose Center for Consolidation and Depulping Center of Jackfruit @ Brgy. Macabug, Ormoc City completed in March 10, 2021, (see JEV no 2021-12-000746 for payment).

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the President of OCJPA prepare the Fund Utilization Report for the fund transferred to the PG totaling ₱1,240,800.00, with complete supporting documents and submit the same to the City Accountant within 15 days from receipt of this report.  We recommended further that Fund Utilization Report for the funds received on December 29, 2020 amounting ₱185,000.00 be prepared and submitted to the OCA within 30 days from utilization of the funds.



We also recommended that the City Accountant prepare the JEV to record the expenditures made by the OCJPA based on the submitted Fund Utilization Report.


Finally, we recommended that the PRDP Focal Person monitor the submission of the required liquidation reports by the OCJPA to the OCA, to ensure that utilization of the funds is duly recorded in the books of accounts of the City and accounts are fairly presented in the financial statements of the PRDP subproject as of yearend.
observations.managementAction:
The OCJPA submitted liquidation amounting to 523,500.00 on May of 2021. It was recorded in books of accounts on May 3, 2021 As per JEV no. 2021-05-000255.

The association submitted additional liquidation documents on November 2021, but is returned to them due to incomplete, and erroneous supporting documents. 

We made follow ups on the remaining balance to be liquidated, but the association submitted the liquidation reports only on February of 2022.

initiate the transfer of cash from the GOP counterpart fund to the LGU Equity account for the disbursements made in excess of the agreed percentage;


instruct the personnel in charge in the preparation of the checks to follow the financing mix provided in the IMA in the subsequent disbursements of the subproject; and


monitor the actual disbursements from the PRDP I-REAP funds to ensure compliance with the relevant provision of the IMA and PRDP Financial Management Operations Guidelines.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Accountant:

require all End-User Units to submit all documents for the procurement of goods charged from the current year appropriation, the obligations of the suppliers having been duly fulfilled, based on the contract, during the current year, to facilitate the recording of the expenses in the books of accounts of the City; and



record in the books of accounts of the City all valid claims chargeable against the appropriations in the year the expenses are incurred and recognize payables for all unpaid expenditures as of yearend.

We also recommended that the City Budget Officer discontinue the practice of charging prior year’s expenditures to the current year appropriation.
observations.managementAction:
Appropriation Ordinance Nos. 036 (for CY2019 expenditures) and 038 (CY2020) were issued to authorize the supplemental appropriation for all obligations not accrued in the books of accounts at the close of the year but were paid in the succeeding years CY2020 and CY2021, respectively. Also attached are copies of the newly issued CY2021 CAFOAs to replace the CY2020 ObRs paid in 2020. (See Annexes F-1-3)

Several meetings were made with end-users, requiring them to submit all relevant documents for the recognition of payables of all unpaid valid obligations as of December 31, 2021.

Unpaid obligations without validated documents submitted to OCA are reverted to the unexpended appropriation at the closing of the books.

observations.recommendation:
We reiterated our recommendation that the Executive Chairperson of the GAD TWG, in coordination with the City Planning and Development Officer, update and make use of the City’s CBMS and existing sex-disaggregated data of the various offices in the City for gender analysis in the preparation of the City’s GAD plan and budget to be able to come up with gender-responsive programs that can address current and relevant gender-related issues.

Also, we recommended that the City Mayor, being the Honorary Chairperson of the Gender and Development Technical Working Group of the City, spearhead the establishment of the City’s GAD Database which will serve as an updated gender statistics and contain information crucial for evidence-based planning and policy-making to properly address current and emerging gender issues.

We recommended that the City Mayor approve and sign NOAs, Contract Agreements and NTPs within the period prescribed in the pertinent provisions of the RIRR of RA No. 9184.  

We also recommended that the BAC Chairperson, through the Secretariat, issue, and post in the PhilGEPs, the NOAs, Contract Agreement and NTPs, in accordance with the period prescribed in the same RIRR.
observations.managementAction:
Systems and procedures to enhance/fast track the signing of documents within the period prescribed under R.A. 9184 have already been introduced and implemented. 

Continuous methods of improvement to streamline and hasten the process is currently ongoing.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor and Accountant comply with COE No. 2015-144 and demand from all persons liable the full payment of the unsettled disallowance totaling ₱4.1 million.
observations.managementAction: In answer to the notices served to liable persons with identified mailing addresses, refund payments totaled to ₱62,030.00 from CY2019 to 2020 and P28,300 in CY2021, given the height of the pandemic.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Mayor, with the assistance of the City Administrator as well as the City Planning and Development Officer, complete the structure to make it operational and utilize the equipment in accordance with the original purpose and in line with the City’s mandate to deliver basic health services.
observations.managementAction:
The building is already fully operational. It is being utilized as a Unified Hospital, manned by the City in partnership with three (3) private hospitals in Ormoc, to serve as isolation facility for symptomatic COVID-19 patients needing constant medical attention.

In 2021, ongoing meetings, planning and preparation are made with respect to the use of the same building as a one stop shop primary health care facility, to include a dialysis center, clinical laboratory, diagnostic radiologic services, pharmacy, birthing services, dental services, and ambulance services, among others, soon after the COVID pandemic subsides.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Mayor should require the Committee of the Housing Program to properly monitor the implementation of its IRR particularly on the imposition of required penalty on past due accounts for recording the same in the books and enforcement of collection thereof.  

Moreover, the Accounting Office should review their records to trace the deficiencies noted to come up with an accurate and reliable account balance in compliance with Sections 2 and 4 of P.D. 1445.  

Further, he should direct offices concerned to exert utmost efforts in the collection of overdue accounts so as to generate funds for the city’s operations.

We recommended that the Cashier, henceforth, prepare and submit to the City Accountant a monthly Schedule of Unreleased Checks.
We also recommended that the City Accountant:
prepare a JEV to restore the unreleased checks based on the schedule prepared by the Cashier to reflect the actual cash balance of the City at the end of the year and to reverse the entries in the ensuing year; and
prepare adjusting journal entries on the identified online collections to its appropriate account.
Furthermore, in order to afford accuracy and reliability of the reported balances of the asset and liability accounts at year end, we recommended for the City Treasurer to:
trace the unidentified online collections or over deposits made and issue an Official Receipt to the payors concerned, if applicable; and
improve the online collections system for easy identification of the clients for the issuance of Official Receipts and recognition of the appropriate income account.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the City Health Office, City SWD Office, City DRRM Office and other offices concerned to:
maintain stock cards for each type of supplies in their possession;
conduct semestral physical count of inventories; and
prepare and submit to the City GS Office the Report of Physical Count of Inventories (RPCI) not later than July 31 and January 31 of each year for the first and second semester, respectively, for consolidation.
Furthermore, we recommended that the City GS Officer maintain supplies stock cards for each type of supply in their custody and submit the consolidated semestral report on physical count of inventories to the City Accounting Office for reconciliation with their records to arrive at an accurate balance of the inventory accounts in the financial statements.
Lastly, we recommended for the City Accountant to stop recording procured supplies as outright expenses, instead, adopt the perpetual method of accounting for inventory, to provide proper monitoring of issuances of inventories and to recognize expenses accurately.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the Chairperson of the Inventory Committee and its members to strictly follow the physical inventory schedule to be able to complete the inventory taking activities and prepare the RPCPPE within the prescribed period.
We reiterated our previous recommendation that the City Accountant and City GS Officer conduct a reconciliation of the PPEs included in the RPCPPE with the total PPE per schedule recorded in the books of accounts of the City and prepare the necessary report on the results of the reconciliation to the City Mayor for his appropriate action for non-existing/missing properties.
Lastly, we recommended that the City Mayor, together with the City Accountant and City GS Officer, perform the procedures to account for non-existing/missing properties as a result of the reconciliation conducted to facilitate the disposition of these items in accordance with the provisions of COA Circular No. 2020-006 to reflect a reliable balance of the related PPE accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the City Accountant:
prepare a JEV to recognize in the books the unrecorded issuances of COVID-19 Rapid Test Kits, Detection Kit and Extraction Kit to correct the overstatement of the balance of the Medical, Dental and Laboratory Supplies Inventory account likewise the understatement of the related expense account to afford fair presentation in the financial statements; and
consistently apply the appropriate inventory method used  in the recognition of issuances of a specific inventory item in the books of accounts same with the concerned Property Officer in recording of the issued item to the end –users,  to correct the noted overstatement of the Drugs and Medicines for Distribution account to afford its fair presentation in the financial statements.
We further recommended, for the adaption of the FIFO inventory method for the issuance of COVID-19 vaccines with known shorter life.
We also recommended that the City Health Officer instruct the concerned Property Officer of the City Health Office to submit the RSMI and RIS of the inventories used/consumed in CY 2021 to the OCA for recording of the issuances in the books of accounts, henceforth, to prepare the RSMI with the RIS and submit the same to the Office of the City Accountant on a monthly basis to facilitate the preparation of the JEV to recognize issuances to arrive at a correct and reconciled balances of the inventory accounts.

We recommended for the President of the Ormoc City Jackfruit Producers Association to immediately prepare and submit the Fund Utilization Report to the OCA for the full settlement of the downloaded funds to OCJPA, to have an accurate balance of the Due from NGOs/POs, Due to NGAs and Due to Other Funds accounts.

We recommended that the City Mayor issue a directive to the Departments/Units concerned, in-charge in the implementation of the programs/projects/activities out of fund transfers received from the different NGAs, to:
fast track the completion of the following long overdue projects:
DA-BFAR –Rehab and Maintenance of Marine Reserve and Fish Sanctuaries;
DA-BFAR-BUB Project Fish Processing, completion of the building; and
iii) 	DA-YRRP –provision of warehouse for the Cassava Dryer and ensure their completion within the first semester of CY 2022;
submit the required Fund Utilization Report and return to the Source Agency the unutilized balance of the following projects:
DA-NMIS,
DOTR; and
DepEd Ormoc;
negotiate from the Dangerous Drug Board for possible fund transfer of additional funds for the completion of the constructed building for AGAK Rehabilitation and Wellness Facility, if not feasible, look for available source of funds of the City that can be utilized for its completion.
We also recommended for the implementing offices or projects in-charge to submit Purchase Requests and/or Program of Works promptly to the City Bids and Awards Committee for on-time commencement of the procurement process and completion of the projects to have the PPAs operational for the achievement of the objectives of the program which ultimately become an additional source of income for the targeted beneficiaries.

We recommended that the City Mayor, through the City Planning and Development Officer and project coordinators, fast track the implementation and completion of the projects in the CY 2021 AIP.  Henceforth, ensure that PPAs in the AIP are implementation-ready which can be implemented and completed during the year, in order to maximize the utilization of the 20% DF to achieve the expected socio-economic and environmental benefits that can be enjoyed by its constituents.

We recommended that the City Mayor, through the City Agriculturist, fast track the processing of the Certificate of Occupancy and have the Multi-Purpose Building installed with electrical connections and water supply and have it ready for the operation of the Jackfruit Enterprise of the City by the OCJPA, for the ultimate contribution as source of additional income to the members of the association.

We recommended that the City Mayor constitute an Inventory Committee and to temporarily relieve the members of the committee of all their regular duties to devote their full time in the conduct of the physical inventory taking until the same is completed pursuant to COA Circular No. 2020-006.

We also recommended that the Chairperson of the Inventory Committee to:

conduct the physical inventory and complete the same before September 30, 2021; and tag all counted properties of the City; and


prepare and submit the RPCPPE on or before January 31, 2022 to the City Accountant and copy furnished the Audit Team and Head of the Property Unit of the City.

We also recommended that the City Accountant and the General Services Officer: 

conduct a reconciliation of the PPEs included in the RPCPPE with the recorded properties in the books of accounts of the City; and

in case non-existing/missing properties will be identified, prepare the necessary report on the results of the reconciliation and to submit the same to the City Mayor for his appropriate action pursuant to Item 7 of COA Circular No. 2020-006.

working papers; 
preparation of RPCPPE on-going

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Accountant:

record all the transactions of the City using the proper accounts provided in the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs so that transactions are faithfully and correctly presented in the financial statements to provide reliable data to the users thereof;

record the utilization of the amount given to the LnB amounting to ₱1,448,389.86 to the proper Government Equity account and record subsequent transfers of funds to the LnB to the Due from Local Government Units account pursuant to COA Circular No. 2015-009; and

Prepare a JEV to reclassify the items recorded in the Other Intangible Assets account to the proper Government Equity account and record similar transactions to the proper expense accounts, unless the criteria for the recognition of Intangible Assets have been fully met as provided for in IPSAS 31.

We recommended that the City Budget Officer require all Departments/Offices to identify the specific items of expenditures to be charged to the Other Maintenance and Operating Expense account during budget preparation and ensure that these expenditures are correctly included in the said account pursuant to COA Circular No. 2015-009.  Alternatively, to propose budget estimates per item of expenditures, based on trending/historical record of actual expenditures.

record goods purchased intended for distribution, in the appropriate Inventory account and be reduced only when distributed based on the RIS and DS submitted monthly by the personnel who has custody of these items pursuant to IPSAS 12.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Accountant:

prepare a Journal Entry Voucher to correct the inventory balance for the medical supplies which were on hand as of December 31, 2020 and record the issued/used Rapid Test Kits and UTM Swab Collection Tubes in CY 2020 based on the RSMI and RIS submitted by the Property Officer of the City Health Department; and


Henceforth, record the purchase of supplies and materials to the appropriate Inventory Account upon disbursement and recognize the related expense accounts only when these are issued/used/consumed based on the RSMI and RIS
We also recommended that the City Health Officer instruct the Property Officer of the Department to submit the RSMI and RIS of the kits used/consumed in CY 2020 to the OCA, and to henceforth prepare the RSMI with the RIS and submit the same to the OCA on a monthly basis to facilitate the preparation of the JEV by the City Accountant to decrease the inventory balances to zero.
observations.managementAction:
Through verbal information from COA Audit Team, OCA immediately prepared JEV no. 2021-05-7190 to record the inventory balance of the UTM Swab Collection Tubes costing P212,500.00.

OCA also prepared JEV no. 2021-05-007206 to record the issuance of inventory items and close the inventory balance to zero, on the basis of the submitted RIS & Distribution Sheets.  

Henceforth, The City Health Property Officer, as instructed,  continue to prepare the RSMI with the RIS and submit to OCA on a monthly basis, as practicable, in order for the latter to decrease the balances on record.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the City Accountant:

compute the unexpended Local DRRM Fund at year end, taking into consideration ALL the disbursements paid and payables recorded in the books of accounts charged from the fund during the year; 



prepare the Local DRRM Fund Utilization Report containing the details of ALL disbursements paid during the month and furnish a copy to the City Budget Office for their reconciliation and adjustment of the amount obligated in the RAO; and


transfer the correct unexpended amount of the Local DRRM Fund every end of each year to the Special Trust Fund account of the City.

We also recommended that the City Budget Officer:

coordinate with the City Accountant and make the necessary adjustments to the RAO- Local DRRM Fund to determine the total unexpended balance of the Local DRRM Fund as of year end and transfer the identified amount to the Local DRRM Fund – Special Trust Fund, to fund the disaster management activities of the City within the next five years and avoid the reversion of the MOOE balance to the surplus of the general fund at the end of the year; and

request for the enactment of an Ordinance to make available the unexpended appropriation for the CY 2020 Local DRRM Fund – MOOE which were reverted to the surplus of the general fund PRIOR TO the payment in CY 2021 of the valid obligations totaling P9,671,127.79 as of December 31, 2020.
observations.managementAction:
Review of the fund was conducted. 
The review and reconciliation resulted to a total of ₱3,220,016.51 which has to be transferred to the special trust fund, while the remaining amount has been set-up as payable or were paid in CY2021 See Annex B.

Said unobligated amount was transferred to
 TF-LDRRM, per OR no. 6578525 dated 8/10/2021 (see JEV No. 2021-08-012919).

On the other hand, Appropriation Ordinance No. 038 series of 2021 was enacted to make available from the adjusted unexpended appropriation a total of Php3,913,848 obligations paid in CY2021 prior to the issuance of the AOM (P3,027,548.00 70% and 886,300 QRF), and CY2021 CAFOAs were subsequently issued to cure the defects. See Annex F-1 & F-2.

Prior to the transfer to the special trust fund account and the enactment of the appropriation ordinance,  adjustments to the RAO were made by the City Budget Office on the basis of the reconciled report from the City Accountant

76

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the Implementing Rules and Regulations of the GAD Code of the City be formulated so that these will serve as basis for identifying programs, activities and projects on GAD. This will also serve as basis to move towards a more sustainable, gender responsive, and performance-based planning and budgeting and strengthening of the GAD Focal Point.
observations.managementAction: Recent revision of the GAD Code (enacted March 3, 2017), for a wider coverage on matters with respect to Gender Awareness and to serve as a better basis for the IRR. On-going preparation of the IRR is currently undertaken.
observations.reason: The IRR of the Ormoc City GAD Code is still being formulated.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the City Mayor and the Sangguniang Panlungsod who represent the City, should cause the review of the assessment whether Tax Ordinances formulated for the purpose were implemented and also cause the undertaking of the General Revision for Real Property Assessments as mandated by law.
observations.managementAction: A “Proposed Schedule of Market Values for the Different Classes of Real Properties in the City of Ormoc” has already undergone a public hearing on April 4, 2018 and is due for a next hearing within the 1st semester of 2018. This revision has been given utmost priority by the Sangguniang Panlungsod.
observations.reason: No revision on real property assessment has been implemented as of December 31, 2017.

observations.recommendation: We recommend for the GSO to carry out the receipt of delivered items and for the GSO to inform the auditor of the undertak ing within twenty-four (24) hours from its execution thru the submission of delivery documents.
observations.managementAction: The General Services Offices has now been continuously in compliance with the required submission of delivery documents within twenty-four hours from the time of acceptance of delivered goods and/or services.
observations.reason: No notices of delivery of procured items has been submitted by the CGSO to the audit team.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the OIC-City Accountant prepare the JEV to record
the amount of P10,127,405.42 in the appropriate accounts and to record the IFRC
donations costing P3,392,001.41 and other donations from government agencies,
private sector or international organizations in the Grants and Donations in Kind
account of the City to provide a reliable balance in its books.

We also recommended that the City Mayor instruct all Department Heads to
submit to the Office of the City Accountant, Invoice Receipts of all properties
donated to the City, immediately after receipt of the items.

We recommended that the City Mayor instruct the Department Heads
handling the programs and projects funded by NGAs to collate and complete
documents necessary to effect payments of delivered equipment/goods as basis for
recording the transactions in the books of the City, and require the OIC-City
Accountant to submit the FUR immediately to the source agency.

We recommended that the Market Administrator, through the City Legal
Officer, initiate the legal measures to collect the receivables amounting to
P7,339,882.24. We recommended further that the lease of all existing and
prospective stallholders be covered by a perfected contract with provisions on the
enforcement of remedies to protect the interests of the City and curtail the
accumulation of receivables.

We also recommended that the OIC-City Accountant and City Treasurer
reconcile their recorded balances on market stall rentals in order to identify the
errors causing the understatement of P4,291,601.69 in the receivable account, and
prepare the necessary adjustments so as to reflect reliable balances in the City’s
books of accounts.

We recommended that the City Development Council, through the City
Mayor as Chairman, evaluate the projects from prior years’ AIP together with the
current year’s identified projects and undertake only those of utmost priority taking
into account the available technical personnel and the number of projects funded by
national government agencies.

We also recommended that the City Mayor approve/issue a Notice of Work
Suspension upon recommendation of the City Engineer only when it is extremely
necessary and supported with sufficient evidence.

We recommended that the City Mayor require the Department Heads who
are designated as Project Coordinators to:
1. Implement each project funded by the National Government in
accordance with the time frame provided in the MOA;
2. Fast-track the implementation of the delayed/unfinished programs and
projects in order to utilize fully the BuB funds from NGAs and ensure the
proper use thereof;
3. Monitor compliance with the provisions in the MOA and the WFP on BuB
programs.

We recommended that the City Mayor, with the assistance of the City
Administrator as well as the CPDO, complete the structure to make it operational
and utilize the equipment in accordance with the original purpose and in line with
the City’s mandate to deliver basic health services.

We recommended that the City Mayor, as Chairman of the CDRRM
Council, through the CDRRM Officer:
1. Monitor quarterly the implemented projects and activities against the
CDRRMF Investment Plan.
2. Re-examine and evaluate the PPAs of prior years, determine the
issues/concerns as to why they were not implemented, and consider them
in the preparation of the Annual CDRRMF Investment Plan.
3. Prepare the CDRRMF Investment Plan with the PPAs therein stated in
specific terms and aligned with the NDRRM Plan.
4. Ensure that the corresponding performance indicators are definite,
measurable, and achievable.

We recommended that the OIC-City Accountant determine the balance of
the goods intended for distribution to the victims of calamities as of December 31,
2017 and prepare a JEV to reclassify this balance to the Welfare Goods for
Distribution account. Thereafter, the OIC-City Accountant and CSWD Officer
should reconcile the balances of the Welfare Goods for Distribution account with the
actual goods on hand for easy monitoring of available goods for distribution to
people affected by calamities.

We recommended that the CSWD Officer:
1. Determine the quantity of goods to be procured based on the number of
victims and their basic needs.
2. Consider the quantity and kind of relief goods which are on stock in the City,
including those which were donated.
3. Include as one of the conditions in the procurement of relief goods, the
expiration dates of at least six months from the delivery date.

We also recommended that the distribution of relief goods be made
exclusively to the rightful recipients pursuant to Section 2 (p) of RA No. 10121.

We recommended that the City Mayor prioritize the provision of an
adequate and proper permanent storage area for all welfare goods donated and
purchased by the City to ensure that the items are safeguarded against any loss and
wastage.

We recommended that the City General Services Officer perform the
following
1. Prepare and accomplish the Acknowledgement Receipt for In-Kind
Donations for DRRM form to document the receipt of all donated relief
goods. All issuances should be supported with the Issuance Form for In-
Kind Donations – DRRM and distribution sheets.
2. Prepare and maintain the Registry of Donated Relief Goods for DRRM for
each kind of relief goods.
3. Prepare the monthly Report on the Receipts and Distribution/Issuances of
Donated Relief Goods duly noted by the City Mayor or his authorized
representative and submit the same to the OCD, Auditor of the OCD and
of the City on a monthly basis until all donated relief goods are fully
distributed to the beneficiaries.
4. Reconcile the balances appearing on the Registry and/or monthly reports
with the stocks on hand and to investigate any differences, if any.

We recommended that the City Mayor, as Chairman of the CDRRM
Council, require the CDRRM Officer to:
1. Prepare the City guidelines on the accreditation, mobilization, and
protection of volunteers for approval of the Council and inclusion in the
Operations Manual of the CDRRMO.
2. Conduct accreditation of volunteers and issue the necessary identification
cards.
3. Provide insurance coverage against injuries/death to accredited
volunteers.

We recommended that the CDRRM Officer conduct an extensive
information/dissemination drive to inform and educate the general public on the
operation protocol of the City Wide Siren and include in the Operation Protocol a
detailed safety/precautionary measures to be undertaken by the public for each type
of hazard/disaster.

We recommended that the LSB (a) re-allocate the lump allocations of P37
million and P4.33 million for specific priority PAPs based on the needs of public
schools, and; (b) include specific PAPs only in the preparation of the Budget for the
Special Education Fund based on the funding requests submitted by elementary and
secondary schools.

We recommended that the Mayor, as Chairman of the LSB, include PPAs for
the ECCD Program in the preparation of the Annual Budget for the SEF to ensure
that the rights and developmental needs of young children will be addressed and
enhanced.

We recommended that the HRMO include in the payroll of the employees
concerned the deduction of loan amortizations based on the order of preference
prescribed in Section 47 of RA No. 10924 otherwise known as the General
Appropriations Act for FY 2017.

We recommended that the Market Administrator comply with the provisions
of the MOA with DENR – EMB RO8 and ensure the implementation of the ESWM
System for the Public Market based on the revised WFP to attain the successful
diversion of solid waste from source to disposal facilities.

We recommended that the Executive Chairperson of the GAD TWG, in
coordination with the CPDO, establish the City’s GAD Database from the CBMS
information for gender analysis in the preparation of the City’s GAD plan to be able
to come up with gender-responsive programs that can address current and relevant
gender-related issues.

We recommended that the members of the OCCWC prepare the Council’s
AWFP before the budgeting schedule and formulation of the AIP to ensure
inclusion, funding and proper attribution of its activities in the Annual Budget and
facilitate implementation of the PPAs to attain the purpose for which the Council
was created.

We recommended that the Market Administrator notify the public of vacant
stalls through extensive postings at the site of the vacant stalls, in the bulletin board
of the office of the Market Administrator and other conspicuous places in the City
to ensure widespread information dissemination of the vacant stalls for lease.

We recommend that the City Mayor instruct the Chairman of the Inventory Committee to conduct a complete annual count of all properties of the LGU and prepare the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant & Equipment (RPCPPE). Also, require the City Accountant to assign a specific personnel who will devote solely on the reconciliation of the records of the Accounting Office against the RPCPPE and prepare the Journal Entry Voucher(s), after reconciliation has been made, to identify the properties costing P306 million and present the accounts fairly in the financial statements.
observations.managementAction: Physical count of PPE was done by GSO and reports have been submitted to OCA, but still subject to verification thru random check test and reconciliati on with accounting records which is still on-going.. Necessary adjustments were made to the accounting records, subject to compliance of procedures for the disposal of all unserviceabl e PPEs. Management shall see to it that annual physical count of PPE and reconciliati on of records shall continuously be implemented and records closely monitored. The maintenance of Property Card for each and every asset/PPE was already implemented.
observations.reason: The reconciliation of the PPE accounts has not been completed. Balances of the specific PPE accounts have not been adjusted.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the City Mayor instruct the City Accountant to submit the lacking JEVs immediately to confirm the validity of the recorded transactions.  Also, require every accounting personnel to monitor all approved and posted JEVs to ensure that all recorded transactions in the books of the LGU have sufficient basis.
observations.managementAction: OCA staff rendered OT services to locate the copies of the JEVs included in The “List of Lacking Vouchers” issued by COA. After thorough checking of the 2nd file copy (OCA file) of all JEVs transmitted, most of the JEVs allegedly not submitted to COA for post audit were actually attached to another JEV since both JEVs shared the same attachment (for example: JEV for collection & the corresponding JEV for its deposit; JEVs to record inventory issuances per week ). Substantial number of JEVs were found and reported to COA.
observations.reason: Most of the JEVs have already been located. However, there were still a few JEVs which have not been submitted.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management Council, through the City Mayor as Chairman, include in the LDRRMF Investment Plan and budget only the projects, programs and activities which are authorized to be funded therefrom.  More importantly, utilize the LDRRMF only for activities for which the fund is intended.
observations.managementAction: Identified PPAs were pursuant to NDRRMC, DBM & DILG JMC Nos. 2013-1 and 2014-1.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the City Mayor and Accountant stop the grant of allowances to individuals who are not teachers and/or handling classes in public schools to avoid disallowances in audit. Also, ensure that the grant of allowances to teachers are existing as of December 31, 1997 pursuant to Item 4(a) of DECS-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No. 1 s. 1998. Also, to stop the grant of allowances to individuals who are not teachers and/or handling classes in public schools to avoid disallowances in audit.
observations.managementAction: Moving forward, the grant of allowances to teachers, with funds charged against the Special Education Fund (SEF) have already been stopped, in compliance the guidelines under DECS-DBM-DILG Joint Circular No.1 s. 1998.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the City Mayor, through the City’s SWM Board, prepare the Solid Waste Management Plan CY 2017-2027 in accordance with the provisions of RA 9003, secure the approval of the NSWMC and ensure its full implementati on.
observations.managementAction: The SWM Plan was submitted to DENR-EMB on Nov. 12, 2017. All plans in the SWM will continuously be implemented in accordance with the Provisions of RA9003 and City Ordinances No. 036.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the City Mayor, and the City General Services Officer (CGSO) improve the implementati on of Solid Waste Management Plan pursuant to RA 9003 through the following: a) adopt a viable and effective collection schedule for specific types of wastes to be collected; b) widen the coverage of the city’s garbage collection system; c) provide personal protective equipment and training on the proper handling of wastes to all collectors/personnel handling solid wastes to ensure their safety during garbage collection; d) acquire additional garbage trucks to implement waste segregation at source and point of collection; e) widen the coverage of the city’s garbage collection system; and f) provide personal protective equipment and training on the proper handling of wastes to all collectors/personnel handling solid wastes to ensure their safety during garbage collection.
observations.managementAction: A viable and effective collection schedule for specific types of wastes to be collected; widen the coverage of the city’s garbage collection system; provide personal protective equipment and training on the proper handling of wastes to all collectors/personnel handling solid wastes to ensure their safety during garbage collection have been implemented. Five (5) additional garbage trucks were purchased to provide a wider and more efficient collection coverage. Now, the City has ten (10) functioning garbage trucks. Further, personal protective equipment are now regularly provided to personnel.
observations.reason: The collection schedule and program is now implemented city wide however full effectiveness is still being enhanced.

observations.recommendation: To correct the deficiencies, we recommend that the City Mayor, the Human Resource Management Officer (HRMO) and the City Accountant to: a.Attach/Submit the duly certified copy of the Job Order Contracts with statement of the expected work or output. b.Require each payee to prepare the Accomplishment Report duly verified and accepted by the immediate supervisor. c.Put up bundy clock to record the time/ man-hours rendered. d.Fill up the existing vacant plantilla positions which are vital in the delivery of basic services which would lessen the number of hired job order workers.
observations.managementAction: Disbursement s for salaries and wages of Job Orders have already been continuously attached with Job Order Contracts/App ointments. Accomplishm ent Reports, and bundy clock punch cards validated punch cards. Fully functioning and regularly maintained bundy clocks are also provided in the entrances of all LGU offices/facilities.
observations.reason: Plantilla positions still remain unfilled. The Job Contracts do not specify the expected output of the workers.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that management require all concerned officials responsible in the granting, utilization and liquidation of cash advances to strictly observe the rules and regulations provided in COA Circular No. 97-002.
observations.managementAction: The Office of the City Accountant has been constantly monitoring liquidation of cash advances and has been continuously implementing the withholding of salaries until submission of liquidation documents and the recording of said liquidation in the books of accounts. Demand letters are also served for unliquidated cash advances or resort to withholding of salaries pursuant to Section 5.1.3 of COA Circular No. 97-002.
observations.reason: Cash advances are still granted despite non liquidation of previous cash advances.

observations.recommendation: We recommend the submission of the required PFPR Disposition Form every quarter and other reports needed for monitoring and evaluation of the program relative to the releases of PFPR/Capitation Fund.  This is necessary to evaluate the effectiveness and efficiency of the PCB 1 package, and to determine if the disbursements conformed to government rules and regulations, policies and Implementing Guidelines of PCB 1. It is further recommended to implement the Projects/Programs/Activities concerning the utilization of the funds under the PFPR on time so that PhilHealth's goal to provide accessible quality health insurance coverage for all Filipinos be achieved as well as to provide the necessary drugs, supplies and services with no out-of-pocket expenses on the part of the members be realized.
observations.managementAction: The mandated 3% per month penalty has already been booked, thus increasing the total receivables to Php1,151,393.25 as at July 31, 2012. Partial payments have been received by the City, reducing the amount of receivable balance to Php235,843.89 as of December 31, 2017.
observations.reason: Cash from the said fund remain unutilized as of December 31, 2017.

observations.recommendation: It is recommended that the City Treasurer and the Market Administrator should cause the City Treasurer to update all subsidiary records pertaining to market rental fees to determine the exact amount of delinquent accounts. The City Treasurer should immediately demand payment from all delinquent stall holders since these accounts have been long overdue. Strictly implement the pertinent provisions of City Ordinance Nos. 67, 70 and 93-01. The Market Administrator should make a monthly report on the status of the Market Stallholders' Rental and update the CTO of delinquent stallholders so that the CTO could institute the proper action.
observations.managementAction: The subsidiary records of market rental fees has been updated and delinquent lessors have already been identified and demanded payment. A monthly report on the Status of Market Stallholders’ Rental is required to be submitted by the Market Administrator for the constant monitoring thereof. As of March 31, 2018, balance of rental payables from rented stalls is Php7,368,387.82. A legal resort to demand for payment of delinquencies shall be implemented.
observations.reason: Subsidiary records have been updated by the CTO however delinquent accounts remain uncollected. A similar observation is contained in Part II of this report.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Treasurer demand immediately payment of all arrearages on rental of stalls and electric consumption. Exhaust all efforts to collect from these delinquent stall holders. It is further recommended to strictly enforce the sanctions stipulated under Section 4.d.9 of Resolution 97-208 and Ordinance 64. The City Treasurer should immediately demand payment and apply remedies such as publication of notices, levy, sale of property and collection through courts as may be necessary.
observations.managementAction: Collection and payment of Superdome rentals and electric consumption has been strictly enforced and subsidiary records of active lessees is duly updated. Contracts for delinquent lessees are terminated and legal action to demand payment has been initiated. The city has currently sold thru auction garnished properties of Arman’s Enterprise in the amount of Php90,000.00 per OR# 5264914 dated May 29, 2017.
observations.reason: Not all receivables have been collected by the LGU.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the City should have its own storage tank considering the need for the storage tank is critical to the City. The cost to hire personnel would be minimal as compared to the benefits it will gain if the control on the issuance of gasoline would already be in the hands of the City.
observations.managementAction: In compliance with the COA recommendations LGU-Ormoc now has its own storage tank for the deposit of diesel fuel procured in bulk. The tanks is located at the GSO compound along Aunubing Street.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the GSD should determine the employee/s accountable of the missing motorcycles and service vehicles. The accountable officer should make a report following Section 151 of COA Circular No. 92-386.
observations.managementAction: The accountable officers of the missing 5 units motorcycles and 2 service vehicles have already been identified. All motor vehicles and one service vehicle were duly returned to City. A lost service vehicle, under the custodianship of retired employee Nelson Bano, was derecognized in the books after payment of its salvage/residual value.
observations.reason: No Notice of loss was filed with the COA nor was a request for relief filed in accordance with COA Circular 92-386.

observations.recommendation: We therefore reiterate the audit recommendation that the City Mayor should issue a memorandum to all accountable officials requiring that all motor vehicles should be on the designated government parking space after office hours and during non-working days.
observations.managementAction: The management has already ordered for the compliance of strict parking of all government motor vehicles at the City Hall parking premises after office hours and when not in use. A constant monitoring is made, subjecting any violating personnel to administrative action.
observations.reason: Only a few motorcycles were found in the premises of the LGU during inspection conducted by the team in March 2018.

observations.recommendation: We recommended that the City Treasurer implement the following provisions in the Local Government Code of 1991 to ensure the collection of delinquent real property taxes: a) Publication of notices of delinquency; b) Levy on real property; c) Sale of property; d) Collection through courts; e) Application of remedies and creation of committee or work force to plan and set in action the strategies to step up collection. Also, monitor the notices of collection sent out by summarizing monthly the results showing the problems encountered and the action taken to serve as input to the committee in evaluating further the effectiveness of the collection strategies employed.
observations.managementAction: 1) The Office of the City Treasurer has constantly caused for the publication of a list of RPT Delinquents in the local newspaper each year. 2) Some obligations have been fully paid while others are paid partially. 3) Balance of delinquent payments is now reduced to Php83,276,945.91 as of January 31, 2018. A continuous action to collect payment pursuant to existing rules and legal procedure is vigorously implemented. It is further recommended that management create a committee or work force to plan and set in action the strategies formulated to step up collection.
observations.reason: Receivables from Real Property Tax have not been fully collected as of December 31, 2017.

observations.recommendation: We recommend strict compliance with relevant laws and rules for every transaction entered into. This policy must be adhered to for payments to be beyond question as to legality as well as regularity. Moreover, specific actions are proposed; viz: 1.) Undertake proper planning and evaluation which should be reasonable and necessary which should suit to a total requirement for which they are to be undertaken. 2.) The Accounting Department should be instructed not to process documents with unauthorized erasures as alterations in public documents are not allowable aside from laying down reasons for the transactions to be questioned in audit. 3.) Further specific actions including return of documents with unauthorized erasures, evaluation of variation orders to affect contract cost etc. should be undertaken.
observations.managementAction: Procurements are authorized only when made in accordance with approved APP/PPMP. The Office of the City Accountant continuously adheres to COA recommendations on not to process documents with unauthorized erasures. Documents submitted with unauthorized erasures are returned accordingly.
observations.reason: Some transactions of the LGU still do not conform with laws, rules and regulations. The irregularities noted were: 1.) Six (6) materials under Item II of the Plans and Specifications were twice listed and priced differently (lists a. to f.). 2.) In the Approved Variation Order, the item one (1) unit 100KVA New Single Phase Distribution Transformer was deleted however the same was not made to effect a decrease in the over-all contract cost. 3.) In the original plan, the airconditioning units to be delivered were 30 units 2.0 HP wall mounted split type and three (3) units 1.0 HP split type for a total price of P2,948,400.00. In the approved Variation Order, a new item of work was introduced described as Item III for Mechanical Works with a total cost of P160,364.70 but without details concerning scope of work. 4.) The Mayor’s Permit showed that the contractor’s classification of business is Services whereas per CIAP the license indicated was for General Engineering and General Building. Thus the distributor of the procured items is not an authorized one, the cost incurred unassured as to reasonableness. 5.) The capacities of the aircon units purchased were more than the need or requirement of the areas/offices in the Agritech Building hence procured quantities are excessive. 6.) The original entry of the Date of Completion on several documents were erased and changed to agree with the Contracted Date of Completion.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that management strictly observe Section 114 of the NGAS Manual for LGUs, Vol. I, for the conduct of reconciliati on on of the account including the effecting of corrections in the books in order to render accurate valuations for said assets in the financial statements. Also, procurement of the school supplies shall be programmed taking into consideration the timeline of a school year for supplies to be consumed according to needs of the targeted students, in effect, avoid the circumstance of overstocking.
observations.managementAction: Monitoring of procured items has been conducted by the Supply Officer.
observations.reason: No complete reconciliation has been conducted between the balance in the books of the LGU and the stocks on hand.

observations.recommendation: We recommend that the City Mayor require the Committee of the Housing Program to properly monitor the implementati on of its IRR particularly on the imposition of the required penalty on past due accounts for recording the same in the books of the Ormoc City Government and enforcement of collection thereof. Moreover, the Accounting Office should review their records to trace the deficiencies noted to come up with an accurate and reliable account balance in compliance with Sections 2 and 4 of PD 1445.
observations.managementAction: The mandated 3% per month penalty has already been booked, thus increasing the total receivables to Php1,151,393.25 as at July 31, 2012. Partial payments have been received reducing the amount of receivable balance to Php235,843.89 as of December 31, 2017.
observations.reason: Receivables still remain uncollected as of year end.

observations.recommendation:
We recommend that the City Mayor instruct the Chairman of the Inventory
Committee to conduct a complete annual count of all properties of the LGU and
prepare the Report on the Physical Count of Property, Plant & Equipment
(RPCPPE).
Also, require the City Accountant to assign a specific personnel who will
devote solely on the reconciliation of the records of the Accounting Office against
the RPCPPE and prepare the Journal Entry Voucher(s), after reconciliation has
been made, to identify the properties costing P306 million and present the accounts
fairly in the financial statements.

We recommend that the City Accountant submit the lacking JEVs
immediately to confirm the validity of the recorded transactions. Also, require
every accounting personnel to monitor all approved and posted JEVs to ensure that
all recorded transactions in the books of the LGU have sufficient basis.

We recommend that the OIC - City Accountant initiate the request for write
off of the dormant accounts pursuant to COA Circular No. 2016-005.

We recommend that the City Development Council, through the City Mayor,
review, update and prioritize the projects in the Annual Investment Plan so that the
funds intended for projects which are no longer feasible can be re-appropriated for
other development projects. Also, plan adequately and thoroughly all
infrastructure projects prior to its inclusion in the Annual Investment Plan to
ensure that only those which can be implemented within two years are included.
Also, we recommend that the City Engineer ensure immediate
implementation and completion of projects within the specified timeframe to
guarantee the full utilization of the 20% Development Fund and attain continuous
development of the city.

We recommend that the City Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council,
through the CDRRM Officer, review the LDRRMIP and include all aspects (proper
fund utilization & identification of PPAs, time table, people, workload, weather and
others) in its preparation in order to accelerate the implementation of projects,
programs and activities within the year and the 70% Mitigation Fund of the
LDRRMF will be fully utilized in accordance with the purpose.

We recommend that the City Disaster Risk Reduction and Management
Council, through the City Mayor as Chairman, include in the LDRRMF Investment
Plan and budget only the projects, programs and activities which are authorized to
be incurred therefrom. More importantly, utilize the LDRRMF only for activities
for which the fund is intended.

We recommend that the City Mayor require the Department Heads/ Project
Coordinators and other personnel involved in the implementation of the projects to
implement all projects within the timeframe specified in the MOA.

We recommend that the City Mayor request the source agencies to include
among the provisions in the MOA the timetable on project implementation to guide
the LGU in its completion. Also, require the City Engineer to closely monitor and
evaluate the implementation of all projects of the LGU in accordance with the
timelines set thereon to ensure the prompt submission of liquidation reports to the
source agencies.

We recommend that the City Mayor through the BAC Chairman submit an
explanation why the supplier was informed and was allowed to demonstrate its
product before the bidding was conducted and prior to his participation therein.
Also, we recommend that the BAC follow strictly the provisions of RA 9184 and its
IRR to ensure regularity and legality of all procurements. Finally, we recommend
that the Heads of Implementing Offices set up the required Signboard at the
project site for all the PPAs of the LGU so that the public is duly notified of the
essential features/elements of the PPAs.

We recommend that the City Mayor and Accountant submit documents to
prove that the grant of allowances to teachers were existing as of December 31, 1997
to ensure that the grant of the same is valid. Also, to stop the grant of allowances to
individuals who are not teachers and/or handling classes in public schools to avoid
disallowances in audit.

We recommend that the Local School Board, through the City Mayor as
Chairman, identify the specific projects, programs and activities for which the
expenditure items in its Budget for Special Education Fund are to be incurred.
Also, utilize the SEF only for activities which the SEF is intended, particularly the
improvement of school facilities.

We recommend that the City Mayor, through the City’s SWM Board,
prepare the Solid Waste Management Plan CY 2017-2027 in accordance with the
provisions of RA 9003, secure the approval of the NSWMC and ensure its full
implementation.

We recommend that the City Mayor and the City General Services Officer
(CGSO) improve the implementation of Solid Waste Management Plan pursuant to
RA 9003 through the following:
a) adopt a viable and effective collection schedule for specific types of
wastes to be collected;
b) acquire additional garbage trucks to implement waste segregation at
source and point of collection;
c) widen the coverage of the citys garbage collection system;
d) provide personal protective equipment and training on the proper
handling of wastes to all collectors/personnel handling solid wastes to
ensure their safety during garbage collection
